title: Don't Pick Winners: Nvidia's Deliberate Neutrality Across the AI Ecosystem

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
One of Nvidia's most counterintuitive strategic principles is its insistence on not picking winners among AI companies, even as it makes billion-dollar investments across the ecosystem. When Huang invests in one foundation model company, he invests in all of them. When he supports one neocloud, he supports many. This neutrality isn't accidental—it's rooted in hard-won corporate memory.

Huang draws the lesson directly from Nvidia's own origin story. When the company started, there were 60 3D graphics companies. Nvidia would have been at the top of the list not to survive—its original architecture was, in Huang's words, "precisely wrong," not slightly wrong but fundamentally incompatible with what developers could support. Yet Nvidia survived and all 59 others didn't. That experience instilled a deep humility about predicting which companies will endure.

This philosophy extends to Nvidia's relationship with cloud providers and neoclouds alike. Nvidia supports CoreWeave, Nscale, Nebius, and others not because it wants to prop them up artificially, but because when these companies come with business plans, expertise, and passion, Nvidia helps them get off the ground. The goal is ecosystem breadth—ensuring AI runs on the American tech stack across as many operators, industries, and geographies as possible.

The "do as much as needed, as little as possible" mantra governs where Nvidia draws lines. It won't become a cloud provider because clouds already exist and others will build them. It won't become a foundation model lab because that's not work only Nvidia can do. But it will build CUDA, NVLink, and domain-specific libraries because without Nvidia doing that work, Huang is completely certain nobody else would have. The company focuses its energy exclusively on work that wouldn't exist without it.

This neutrality creates a powerful dynamic: every AI startup, every cloud, every enterprise can trust that Nvidia isn't secretly competing with them or favoring their rival. That trust is what allows Nvidia to occupy the center of a trillion-dollar ecosystem without the centrifugal forces that typically tear apart platform companies that compete with their own customers.
